english: cotton; acc. to others velvet. — dar-dmán-pa (dar dman pa) raw silk Schr. — dar-tsón-pa (dar tson pa) a dealer in silks, a silk-mercer. — dar-záb (dar zab) the finest silk, frq.; a piece of such silk. — dar-yáb (dar yab) a silk fan. — dar-yúg (dar yug) a narrow ribbon-like piece of silk-stuff Glr., Mil. — dar-liṅ (dar ling) = *dhar-ma-re* (*dhar ma re*). — dar-śám (dar sham) the lower border of a silk dress Glr. — dar-(gyi) sriṅ(-bu) (dar (gyi) sring (-bu)) silk-worm. — 2. a cloth, made of whatever material; flag Wts., sail (v. yyór-mo (g.yor mo)); °pyar-dár ('phyar dar) a hoisted flag; mduṅ-dar (mdung dar) a little flag fixed to a lance; *ru-dhár* (*ru dhar*) C. military banner. — dar-lčóg (dar lcog) little flags fixed on houses, piles of stones, and the like (v. Schl. Buddh. 198). — dar-po-čé (dar po che) 1. a large flag fastened to a flag-staff; 2. flag-staff, mast. — dar - tsó (dar tsho) a military division, squadron Sch. — dar-šiṅ (dar shing), dar-bér (dar ber), prob. flag-staff. II. ice, icy plain; dar čágs (dar chags) ice is forming; also substantively = dar (dar), mtsó-la dar-čágs btab (mtsho la dar chags btab) Mil. — dar-zám (dar zam) ice-bridge. — *dar-jár* (*dar jar*) ('clinging to the ice'?) W. a dark-gray aquatic bird. III. v. dar-yčíg (dar gcig), dár-ba (dar ba), dár-ma (dar ma).

(col. also dal-yčíg (dal gcig)), a little while, a moment; dar-yčíg lón-pa-na (dar gcig lon pa na) after a little while Glr.; adverbially: for a little while, for a moment Mil.: directly, instantly, in a moment Mil.; dár-tsam (dar tsam) Sch. id.
humming, buzzing Mil.; wailing, lamenting Pth
grinding-stone for Indian ink Sch.; bdár-rdo (bdar rdo) would perhaps be more correct.
col. for dál-po (dal po), dál-mo (dal mo), v. dál-ba (dal ba).

I. sbst., also dá-ra (da ra), dar (dar), buttermilk, dar-gsár (dar gsar) fresh buttermilk. II. vb. 1. to be diffused, to spread, of influence, power, opinions, diseases, čes dár-ba (ces dar ba) to gain much ground, to increase exceedingly Lt.; dár-du °júg-pa (dar du 'jug pa) (act.) to extend, enlarge, e.g. academies Glr.; dar-gúd (dar gud) spreading and decaying, increase and decrease; *dhár-po* (*dhar po*) C. grand, magnificent, of a feast, drinking-bout. — 2. with lag (lag), to take in hand, to put hand to a work, c. la (la) Dzl.; also dáṅ-ba (dang ba).

1 the age of manhood, manly age, prime of life, gen. reckoned from 30 to 50, but acc. to S.g. from 16-70; dár-la báb-pa (dar la bab pa), or dar-báb (dar bab), a person in the prime of life, frq.; dar-gáṅ (dar gang) col. id.; dar-yól (dar yol) a person beyond that age. — 2. a man, and dár-mo (dar mo) a woman in the prime of life.
Mil., rims-dál (rims dal) Mil., epidemic disease, plague, or perh. n. of a particular disease.
to attack and disperse an enemy Sch.

slowness, ease, quietness, leisure (opp. to haste, hurry, vehemence), *dhál-wa* (*dhal wa*) (or dhál-bu (dhal bu)) yö'-dham* (yo' dham*) C., have you time? dál-ba žíg-gi skábs-su (dal ba zhig gi skabs su) when he happened to have nothing to do Dzl.; dál-bar °dúg-pa (dal bar 'dug pa) to be disengaged, unemployed; dál-ba brgyad (dal ba brgyad) the eight conditions of rest, the state of being free from the eight mi-kóm-pa (mi khom pa); to these belong the °byor-pa bču ('byor pa bcu), i.e. ten goods or blessings which, in part, are but more particular definitions of the eight rests, yet include also other blessings; hence both together are called dal-°byór bčo-brgyád (dal 'byor bco brgyad) (another instance of this peculiar way of reckoning v. sub nyin-mtsán (nyin mtshan)) As these various conditions are partly characteristics of 'humanity', and attainable only by human
